spec_match.rationale: Tables of qualification criteria match the explainer well, but the chromestatus summary calls out specific resource categories (analytics scripts, social embeds, video players, captcha, ads libraries) that are absent from the page.

interactive.rationale: The only 'live' surface is a generic 'Run capability probe / Toggle comparison state' panel copy-pasted from another concept — it probes unrelated APIs (CSS.supports, navigator.gpu, customElements) and has nothing to do with cache sharing. This is the exact static-content-with-fake-interactivity anti-pattern flagged in CLAUDE.md.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises 'what makes a resource extremely pervasive, which resources qualify, and the privacy/performance tradeoff' — the criteria and tradeoff are there, but 'which resources currently qualify' is glossed over with no examples.

edge_cases.rationale: The non-qualifying table is good but misses edge cases the spec actually wrestles with: versioned URLs where versioning is manual (excluded per chromestatus), content-dictionary resources (explicitly included), and the cookie-Vary interaction.

references.rationale: Links to https://chromestatus.com/feature/5202380930678784 in the see-also section.

design_system.rationale: All colours via CSS variables, criteria tables use accent-emerald/accent-rose for yes/no with sufficient contrast, no button-colour regression.

openQuestions.title: Replace the generic live probe with a real allowlist matcher
openQuestions.detail: The current 'Run capability probe' is unrelated to cache sharing and is a regression from the static-card prohibition in CLAUDE.md. Replace it with a textarea where authors paste a URL or fetch entry and the page tells them whether it would qualify based on the criteria table — that's a real interactive surface for this concept.

openQuestions.title: List representative qualifying resource categories
openQuestions.detail: Chromestatus explicitly enumerates: 'common analytics scripts, social media embeds, video player embeds, captcha providers and ads libraries' — and excludes self-hosted/version-pinned library URLs. Add a 'qualifies / does not qualify' worked-example table built from those exact categories so authors know which third-party URLs in their pages are affected.

openQuestions.title: Explain the HTTP Archive curation step
openQuestions.detail: Chromestatus says the allowlist is 'curated from the HTTP Archive dataset'. That is the answer to 'how do I get my CDN on the list?' and deserves a paragraph — describe the HTTP Archive query, the cadence of curation, and where developers can see/influence the list.

openQuestions.title: Tie the privacy tradeoff to a concrete cache-timing attack
openQuestions.detail: The tradeoff card claims 'cache timing reveals presence/absence — but these resources are always present' as the safety argument. Concretise it: link to or summarise the 2014 Smith/Snyder paper on cache-timing attacks so the reader understands what was prevented in Chrome 86 and why this carve-out is safe.

summary: Solid reference tables but the page ships the generic copy-pasted 'live probe' that has nothing to do with cache sharing — a clear regression against the no-static-card rule. Replace it with a real qualify/disqualify matcher and add the resource-category examples the chromestatus summary calls out.
